ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for nasi lemak rice; closest parent is cooked rice food product (FOODON_00002694).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers cooked rice generically; no specific nasi lemak coconut rice term exists in FoodEx2. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for nasi lemak rice or coconut-cooked rice as a distinct clinical food concept.

serving.description: 1 serving of nasi lemak rice (ala carte portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical ala carte serving of nasi lemak rice is approximately 150 g, equivalent to roughly one cup of cooked coconut-infused rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (Rice only component) — adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists full nasi lemak (with accompaniments). The rice-only component was isolated by referencing the HPB entry for plain nasi lemak rice and cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA coconut rice values. Macros reflect the cooked rice base prepared with coconut milk, pandan, and salt, without sambal, egg, or anchovies. Energy calculated from Atwater factors. Sodium reflects salt added during cooking.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for coconut milk rice / jasmine rice cooked with coconut milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for nasi lemak rice (coconut milk rice) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: white rice cooked with coconut milk, which has been shown to lower the GI of plain white rice (GI ~72) due to the fat content of coconut milk slowing gastric emptying and starch digestion. This is consistent with published data in Atkinson et al. (2021) and peer-reviewed studies on fat-enriched rice preparations. GL per serving = (54 × 42.75 g available carbohydrate per 150 g serving) / 100 = 23.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salt added during cooking
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving of nasi lemak rice contributes approximately 270 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily sodium intake and limit additional high-sodium accompaniments such as sambal or salted fish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (54) and high carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the fat from coconut milk moderates the GI to approximately 54 (low-medium boundary), the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 23, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 100–120 g cooked rice and pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Nasi lemak rice derives most of its fat from coconut milk, which is rich in medium-chain saturated fatty acids (approximately 4.5 g saturated fat per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and be mindful of cumulative saturated fat intake across the full nasi lemak meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak Rice is the fragrant coconut-milk-infused rice that forms the centrepiece of the nasi lemak dish, a beloved staple across Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore. The rice is prepared by cooking long-grain or jasmine white rice in a mixture of coconut milk and water, typically with pandan leaves, lemongrass, and a pinch of salt added to the pot to impart a subtle floral, creamy aroma. The result is a slightly sticky, richly fragrant rice with a mild coconut flavour and a soft, tender texture. When served ala carte — as in this field survey listing from Smile Martabak — the rice is presented without the traditional accompaniments (sambal, fried anchovies, cucumber, and hard-boiled egg), making it a versatile base component. Nutritionally, the rice provides approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (~28.5 g/100 g) and a meaningful saturated fat contribution (~4.5 g/100 g) from the coconut milk. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 54 — lower than plain white rice — because the fat in coconut milk slows starch digestion. Clinically, the moderate GI and high glycemic load per serving make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, while the saturated fat content warrants attention for those with cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal.

meal_context: base component of nasi lemak set meal

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS entry exists specifically for the rice-only component of nasi lemak as served ala carte. Values are adapted from the HPB nasi lemak full-dish entry and FAO/INFOODS SEA coconut rice data by isolating the rice base. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on fat-enriched rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values reflect the rice component only (cooked with coconut milk, pandan, lemongrass, salt) and exclude all nasi lemak accompaniments (sambal, anchovies, egg, cucumber). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as coconut milk and rice contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based rice dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; white rice and coconut milk contain no meaningful vitamin C. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no animal products present. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no vitamin D sources present in this plant-based rice preparation. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not separately reported for the rice-only component in the adapted source. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this preparation. 9. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this preparation. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this preparation. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this preparation. 12. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for nasi lemak rice is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 13. The cuisine tag in the input JSON lists 'Indonesian'; nasi lemak is also a canonical Malaysian and Singaporean dish, but the Indonesian classification is preserved per the source record. 14. Serving size of 150 g is an estimate for a typical ala carte rice portion; actual restaurant portion may vary.
